title: Water treatment coursework — wetlands, sustainability triple-bottom-line
html:
Water treatment coursework — wetlands, sustainability triple-bottom-line

Source: 2022-04-21 Apple Note. Class notes from what looks like the OSU GEOG203 Human Environment Geography course (spring 2022) — the Albany wetlands case study.

TL;DR

  • Q&A-format study notes on municipal water treatment via constructed wetlands: nutrient removal, oxygen via waterfalls, plant criteria (native + wet-tolerant + water-cooling).
  • "Resilient plant community" defined: survives all seasons + adapts to environmental pressure + disease-resistant.
  • Sustainability triple-bottom-line analysis for the wetlands: environmental (carbon cycle, flood control, salmon temp regulation, but invasive species + mosquitoes + leaks); social (scenic + 3rd space, but allergies + animal density + traffic); economic (property value increase + city avoids fines, but expensive + revenue-negative).
  • Embedded gentrification observation: "property levels went up, values went up. (Could be positive for some but negative to those who are lower social class and can't afford to live anymore)" — early framing of displacement via amenity economics.
  • One of the rare pure-coursework notes that survives in the corpus — most class notes are absent.

Pattern observed

This is the only sustained engineering/environmental coursework note in the apple-notes corpus. Worth bookmarking because it shows Yahir DID engage academically in geog203-style sustainability frameworks before the CS-major pivot consumed his attention. The triple-bottom-line lens (environmental / social / economic) shows up later in Rankett's 12 month plan (revenue ladder + service tier + non-negotiables).

Open questions

  • Was this for an exam? A class assignment?
  • The Albany wetlands — Albany, OR or somewhere else?

Related

  • osu
  • gentrification
  • sustainability triple bottom line
  • geog203
